coredata.dc:description: Citrazinic acid is an important substance because of its applicability and its role as a chemical precursor. The synthetic section of this review stresses the formation of the product from citric acid esters. Tautomerism and acidity have been studied extensively. The reaction-based section emphasizes transformations of the carboxyl group and such at 2/6 and 3/5 positions (including the synthesis of azo dyes). Among applications, use in pharmacy, analysis, and in materials and processes deserve most attention.

head.ce:label: Chapter One
head.ce:title: The chemistry of citrazinic acid (2,6-dihydroxyisonicotinic acid)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Citrazinic acid is an important substance because of its applicability and its role as a chemical precursor. The synthetic section of this review stresses the formation of the product from citric acid esters. Tautomerism and acidity have been studied extensively. The reaction-based section emphasizes transformations of the carboxyl group and such at 2/6 and 3/5 positions (including the synthesis of azo dyes). Among applications, use in pharmacy, analysis, and in materials and processes deserve most attention.

ce:section.ce:para: Citrazinic acid (CZA, 1 in Scheme 1 ) has attracted considerable interest because of its various applications and its role as a keynote intermediate in the synthesis of important chemical substances. No review seems to exist until now. Early results published before 1960 have been included in pyridine-related chapters of Rodd's 1 and Weissberger's 2–5 series.

This chapter reviews relevant literature published from 1960 through 2020 together with most of the work made public in 2021. It also takes into account carboxylic acid as well as 2/6-O-functional derivatives of CZA. For the purpose of completeness, it is sometimes necessary to include 2/6-halogenated derivatives that are the most frequent consequential products in the CZA chemistry. Patents are mentioned provided they reveal additional important details of synthesis or application.

In this review, for consistency, parent substance CZA is generally drawn and numbered as a lactam of formula 1. In the literature, however, the substance is often described and drawn as a tautomeric 2,6-dihydroxy (2) or (less frequently) 2,6-dioxo (3) structure. CZA derivatives will usually be depicted in the forms used by the respective authors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: From citric acid or its trialkyl esters
ce:section.ce:para:
In the same way as described in earlier reports (cf. Refs. 1–4), in the period under review nearly every CZA synthesis has been based on citric acid or its trialkyl esters, partly using drastic conditions. Scheme 2 shows the basic reaction chain that leads, via intermediates 5 and 6 (either isolated or non-isolated), to CZA. Published variants make use of citric acid (4a) 6 or its trialkyl esters (4b) 7
,
8 and ammonia or of citric acid (or esters) and urea. 9
,
10 A similar procedure 11 gives a 1:3 mixture of CZA and amide 6.
The synthesis has been applied 12 to labeled citrazinic acid-2,6-14C, formed from trimethyl citrate-1,5-14C.

There is a weak point of the basic reaction, in so far as the central carboxyl group of citric acid takes part in the amidation, and the resulting CZA amide (6) has to be hydrolysed, 13 yield and purity of the product being diminished. Colored raw products, that are sometimes described as “paste” or “reddish brown,” 12 have to be purified. 8
,
14

ce:section.ce:section-title: From symmetric citric acid dialkyl esters
ce:section.ce:para: A useful alternative synthetic route leading to CZA is based on symmetric dialkyl citrates 15 instead of trialkyl citrates, thus avoiding amide hydrolysis and any purification.

Precondition is an efficient, selective esterification of citric acid. The required diesters had originally been isolated as by-products of triesters. It was found, however, that they can be prepared smoothly by esterification in the presence of an acidic cation exchanger. 16 The dimethyl ester is the best choice, because it is obtained as a crystalline monohydrate (7), avoiding ester distillation. Amidation then leads to ammonium salt 8, subsequent cyclization directly to analytically pure CZA.

The cyclization reactions of acetylenedicarboxylate 9 (Scheme 3 ) with nitriles, enamines, and related compounds offer access to several ring substituted CZA derivatives. This way, the interaction of malonitrile affords (beside an acyclic adduct) 3-cyano compound 10. 17 Furthermore, condensation with enamine 12 or isocyanate 14 leads to dicarboxylic esters 13
18 and 15a, 19 respectively. In the latter case, the second silylation is explained to be the reaction of a primary adduct with any silylating reagent present in the mixture. Another dicarboxylate (17) is formed in the course of a [4+2] cycloaddition of pyrimidine 16 by recyclization. 20
Finally, CZA nitrile-type substances have been obtained from tetracyano ketones. 21

The introduction of 4-carboxyl functionalities into 4-unsubstituted pyridine derivatives 18 and 20 is shown in Scheme 4 . Metalation of substance 18 in the ortho-position to the carbamate group and subsequent addition of chloroformate gave CZA ester 19a. 22 The formation of CZA diether 21 by the direct addition of carbon dioxide was claimed. 23
An oxidative bacterial metabolism of isonicotinic acid (22) was repeatedly described to proceed via 2-hydroxyisonicotinic acid (23) and CZA 24–28 (cf. Section 4.6).

Among several quantum chemical calculations, a recent and detailed study 29 describes results on the structure, spectroscopy, and molecular docking of CZA, the theoretical calculations being done by the DFT method.

The crystal structure of the polymeric Co(II) complex of CZA bound to pyridine, 30 of CZA diether 171 complexed with trypsine, 31 and of CZA nitrile tetraether 98
32 were determined.

Few NMR signals of ring-attached hydrogen atoms are available in CZA derivatives (Table 1 ). 13C NMR data are listed in Table 2 . Both these spectra of CZA each were recently reproduced, tabulated, and discussed. 29
EPR analysis and DFT computation of tetranitroxide 98 and related compounds were described. 36

Absorption data of CZA and derivatives in the UV–vis region are shown in Table 3 . (Regarding CZA, see additionally recent work. 29 ) CZA is a weakly fluorescent compound the optical properties of which have been found to depend on aggregation states and chemical environment, especially the pH value, because tautomerization and protonation are influenced. 37
,
40
,
41 In acidic conditions a marked blue shift of the absorption is observed. On lowering the pH value from 14 to 2 the color of the solution changes from yellow to brown.

Increasing CZA concentration likewise causes a blue shift, typically ascribed to the formation of oligomers. 42 CZA is sensitive to UV exposure and undergoes photobleaching, the intensity of absorption and fluorescence being reduced. 43
Remarkable absorption in the visible region is shown by dark-red Mannich base 137. 44

Assigned IR bands of the CZA ring system in selected substances are gathered in Table 4 . The IR spectra of CZA and azo compound 156 were fully discussed. 41 The broad nature of the NH band indicates that this group participates in a hydrogen bond structure. The spectra of CZA esters as well as 2,6-O-functional and deuterated derivatives were studied. 45

Normal Raman and SERS spectra were described. 40
,
42 FT-IR and FT-Raman spectra of CZA were presented and discussed in detail. 29

The initial steps of the mass spectrometric fragmentation of 3(o-tolylazo) CZA (24) 46 are depicted in Scheme 5 . The higher intensity of fragmentation route A rather than B indicates the different strengths of the relevant CN bonds.

The separation and determination of CZA by HPLC was described. 47 The detection of CZA and its amide by paper and thin-layer chromatography 48 make use of the reaction with p-dimethylaminobenzaldehyde which forms colored products 25a and b,
a
a In the captions beneath the formulas, substituents (R or X) in parentheses refer, in the order given, to substructures a, b, c, etc. of the respective formula or all formulas of the reaction. respectively.

Polarographic halfwave potentials were reported of amides (e.g., 41 and 101) of the 2,6-dichloro derivative and of 2/6-O- or –N-functional analogs (e.g., 102 and 103). 49 The electrochemical behavior (DC-polarography and cyclic voltammetry) and thermodynamic parameters (activation energy, enthalpy change, and entropy change) of CZA azo dyes (especially 4′-nitro derivatives, e.g., 155b) were studied. 50

The lactam tautomer 1 of CZA is expected to be the predominant form in solutions, 29 but possibly CZA binds as lactim 2 if that hydrogen bond arrangement is more favorable. 51 Tautomer 2 is predominant in acidic media. 41 Another special property of CZA is dimerization, for instance, by means of H-bridges. 29

CZA alkyl esters have been proved to exist in ethanol extensively in the lactam form (analogs of 1), in nonpolar solvents, however, in the dioxo form (analogs of 3) 45 (cf. Ref. 52). In the case of 3-octanamido CZA (30b) the dioxo and (in acidic solution) the lactim forms are proposed. 33

Regarding CZA azo dyes (e.g., 155a
38 and 158
34 ) an additional phenyl-azo-phenylhydrazino tautomerism has to be considered.

The dissociation constants of CZA and its methyl ester 14 and of its mono- and bis-azo compounds 34
,
38 were determined. The pK a values of the carboxyl and the hydroxyl group of CZA have been observed to be 4.0 and 11.04, respectively. 38 Depending on the pH value, the molecule exists in various protonated and/or deprotonated forms. 40
,
41

Salts 26 and 27 (Scheme 6 ) were produced by neutralizing CZA with tetrabutylphosphonium or –ammonium hydroxide, using appropriate molar ratios. 53 The syntheses of two bimetal salts of CZA have been claimed: ZrIVCuII (C6H2NO4)2·2 H2O 54 and the analogous ZrIVPbII salt. 55

Metal complexes of CZA and its derivatives, especially of some selected azo compounds, are listed in Table 5 . Moreover, a hydrazide chelate complex compound of cobalt (1:2) was claimed. 67 Finally, a large cage compound of gallium 68 and inclusion complexes based on CZA and imidazoles (e.g., 28) 69 should be mentioned.

A simple esterification affords CZA methyl ester 29 (Scheme 7 ) the hydrochloride of which is stable in the absence of water. 14 Under other conditions, the reaction may be accompanied by a 2,6-di-O-alkylation to give, for instance, ester 31
33 (cf. Ref. 70). The CZA esterification can proceed in situ together with a halogenation in 2,6 positions leading, for example, to esters 32 and 33. 35
,
71 CZA was even esterified with hydroxymethyl polystyrene resin 34, 72 adsorbent 35 being obtained. b
b More examples of citrazinic esters and amides will be put together in tabular form (see below).

The transformation of ester 36 to amide 37 and thioamide 39
73 is depicted in Scheme 8 . Amidation via (not isolated) acid chloride is exemplified by the formation of substance 41
74 (cf. Ref. 35). The reaction of ester 32 and m-anisidine unexpectedly resulted in an amidation (to give anisidide 42) instead of amination in the 2 position. 35 CZA chloride reacted with p-phenylenediamine to afford bis-CZA amides (such as 43a) 75 whereas triethylenetetramine formed a tris-CZA amide. 76 The synthesis of pyridyl- and thiazolyamides and their conversion by Lawesson's reagent into thioamides (e.g., 43b) was claimed. 77

Among other more complex amide structures there are substituted benzylamides, 78 piperid-4-ylamides, 79 and secondary amides, 80 specially piperid-1-yl-, 81 and tetrahydroqinolin-1-ylcarbonyl compounds. 82 Additional examples of CZA ester and amide syntheses are listed in Table 6 .

CZA hydrazide (71) is smoothly obtained from the methyl ester (29). 89 The analogous reaction of monomethyl ether 44 and subsequent reactions of hydrazide 45 to give hydrazone 46 and hydrazine 47
90 are shown in Scheme 9 . 2,6-Di-O-butylated CZA chloride (48) and the respective methyl ester (50) reacted in different ways with methylhydrazine and produced isomeric hydrazides 49 and 51, respectively. 91 CZA hydrazide (71) and pyromellitic anhydride gave (partially cyclic) bis-hydrazide 52. 92

CZA hydroxamates (such as 54, Scheme 10 ) are prepared from CZA ethers (e.g., 53) 93 (cf. Ref. 22) or their esters 7
,
91 by the Weinreb amide synthesis. These hydroxamates are precursors of Mannich bases, for instance, of protected Mannich base 55. Similar reaction chains of CZA ethers, 94
,
95 their ester, 96 and a thioether 23 have been described.

Nitriles of CZA and derivatives may be obtained when dehydrating carbonamides (e.g., 38 from 37), 73 and vice versa they are transformed to amides. A special example makes use of biocatalysis by cobalt nitrile hydratase (Nhase), producing amide 57 from nitrile 56. 97

CZA chlorides (e.g., 48 or 58
98
,
99 ) are frequently used intermediates (either isolated or non-isolated) in transformations of the carboxyl group.

The Grignard reaction transforms CZA derivatives (e.g., 40, Scheme 11 ) into 4-acylpyridines (such as 59, 100 cf. Ref. 101), whereas 4-cabamidoacylpyridine 61 is prepared from ester 60. 102 Reduction of nitrile 62 gives pyridinealdehyde 63 and by-product 64. 103 The synthesis of relevant Mannich bases was just described (Section 4.2.1, Scheme 10).

CZA (or its esters), derivatized in 2,6 positions, are usually reduced to 4-hydroxymethylpyridines (e.g., 66, 67, and 68, Scheme 12 ) by borane (or adducts), 104 LiAlH4
86 or NaBH4. 71 Product 68 was also obtained by an in situ bromination/reduction of CZA. 105
Ester 33 was converted to 4-isopropenylpyridine 69 by a Grignard reaction followed by an elimination. 106
The reaction of CZA with phenylphosphonic dichloride and PCl5 afforded 4-trichloromethylpyridine 70. 12

The carbon atom of the CZA carboxyl group may become part of a heterocyclic ring (Scheme 13 ). One route of transforming CZA to 4-heterocyclylpyridines is based on the cyclization of CZA hydrazide (71). This way, triazolyl- (72) and oxadiazolylpyridines (73) were synthesized 92 (cf. Ref. 107), furthermore thiadiazolyl compounds. 89 In another route the carboxyl group reacts with o-disubstituted carbo- or heterocyclic compounds (74 or 76, resp.) and serves to close a fused second ring. This way, 4-benzazolylpyridines 75a–c
108 and 4-(triazolothiadiazolyl)pyridines (e.g., 77) 109–111 were synthesized.

The synthesis of 4-aminopyridines from CZA derivatives (via non-isolated intermediates) is exemplified by the transformation of amide 79 into amine 80 (Scheme 15 ) using a Hofmann rearrangement 113 and of carboxylic acid 40 into amine 81 by a Curtius rearrangement 114 (cf. Refs. 115, 116). A variant of the latter method leads, after trapping the intermediate isocyanate 82 by alcohols or amines, to carbamates (e.g., 83) 117 (cf. Ref. 98) or ureas, 118 respectively.
The decarboxylation of CZA is claimed to proceed at 100°C 119 whereas azo derivative 148 was treated at 240°C/0.5Torr. 120 The rate constant of decarboxylating CZA was determined at 230°C. 121

Examples of direct etherification in both 2 and 6 position (Scheme 16 ) include the formation of dioleyloxy derivative 85 (via 84) 70 and the Mitsunobu reaction between ester 29 and benzyl alcohol 86 to give dibenzyl ether 87 and finally a macrocyclic dye. 122

Esterification in 2,6 positions proceeds by means of acetanhydride 45 or acetylchloride/pyridine. 119 Furthermore, bis-carbonate 89
123 and bis-sulfonate (specially triflate) 90
83 were described.

2,6-Dichloro (40) and (less frequently) 2,6-dibromo derivative (92) of CZA (Scheme 17 ) are, because of their reactivities, the mostly used intermediates in the CZA chemistry. The halogenation preferentially proceeds by means of phosphorus oxyhalides (e.g., 124 ); diiodo compound 91 is indirectly accessible. Among catalysts there are 2,6-lutidine, 39 bis(trichloromethyl)carbonate, 125 and especially one equivalent of tetra-methylammonium chloride. 100 This way, the application of pressure (cf. Ref. 7) can be avoided. Dichloride 40 may also be prepared by the use of phenylphosphonic dichloride (PhPOCl2). 99

There are in situ syntheses enabling combined halogenation plus esterification, 126 for example, to produce ester 32.
35 The analogous dibromo ester 33 was inexpensively obtained by the treatment of CZA with POBr3 (which was generated in situ from a mixture of PBr3, bromine, and P2O5), then with methanol. 127
This section gives an overview of reactions that make use of the reactive CZA derivatives mentioned above.

The synthesis of bis-ethers is shown in Scheme 18 . Thus, dichloride 40 is transformed into bis-alkylether 53
86 and bis-arylether 94
128 ; bis-heterocyclylether 95 is similarly obtained 129 (cf. Ref. 130). Tetrafluonitrile 96 with TEMPOL (97) undergoes nucleophilic substitutions at 2,6 and additionally at 3,5 positions to deliver tetraether 98. 32
Bis-thioethers (such as 93) are synthesized analogously to bis-ethers. 96

2,6-Bis-amination or CN linking heterocyclyl substitution of dichloride 40 (Scheme 19 ) lead, for instance, to diamine 99
131 and diimidazolyl compound 100, 72 respectively. Amide 101undergoes, depending on the conditions, selective mono- or uniform bis-amination. 49

The substitution by carbon functions at 2 and 6 positions is summarized in Scheme 20 . The Fürstner reaction transforms ester 104 into dimethyl derivative 105 whereas Suzuki coupling (using cyclotriboroxane 106) leads to divinyl compound 107. 85 Ester 33 is converted by means of Sonogashira coupling into diethynyl derivative 108. 132

In the field of aromatic substitutions the syntheses of diarylpyridine 109
114 and (by Stille coupling) of oligopyridines, such as terpyridine 111
133 and quinquepyridines, 134 should be mentioned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and direct substitution at 2 or 6 position
ce:section.ce:para:
The selective etherification of CZA (Scheme 21 ) was combined with an esterification and gave rise, for instance, to monoether 44
90 and, in a second step, to unsymmetrical bis-ethers 112
104 (cf. Ref. 77) or 113. 135 In another monomethylether the hydroxyl group was protected by a p-methoxybenzyl (PMB) group. 93

The substitution reactions of, for instance, dichlorides 101 (see Scheme 19) or 40 (Scheme 22 ) under special, milder conditions lead to monochlorinated amine 102
49 or ether 114 and thioether 115, 136 respectively. The halogenation of CZA and subsequent monosubstitution may proceed in situ (thus affording ether 116
137 ) and in addition to that may be combined with an esterification (e.g., to give 118). 113 Some further reactions are listed in Table 7 , Part A.

A second nucleophilic reaction of the resultant monohalo derivatives usually attacks the halogen substituents (see Table 7, Part B). A further example is unsymmetrical bis-ether 120a
51 (Scheme 23 ). Such a substitution may also take place in situ, together with an esterification, as shown in the case of nitrile 117. 137 Finally, it is possible to substitute the halogen atoms of a 2,6-dihalo precursor one after the other selectively, but in one in situ reaction, as explained by the synthesis of bipyridine 123.
140

In the field of ether cleavage, the deprotection of mixed bis-ether 120a (to give monoether 120b) 51 and the desilylation of dicarbonester 15a
19 were depicted above. Typical cleavage reactions (Scheme 24 ) concern the CZA N-oxide bis-ethylether (126) 141 and the respective bis-benzylether. 142 Ether 126 could be cleaved to afford 1-hydroxy CZA (127), but on the other hand it could (like the bis-phenylether) be selectively cleaved to give monoethylether 129. 142 This ether and other monoalkylethers may be directly obtained from acid 128.
A transetherification of CZA nitrile 95 produced mixed bis-ether 131. 129 Finally, dichloride 40 in a partial elimination reaction formed 2-chloroisonicotinic acid (132). 125

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactions at the ring nitrogen atom
ce:section.ce:para:
An overview of 1-substituted CZA derivatives regarding N-oxides 141
,
142 and N-alkyl compounds 45 can be found in Schemes 24 (Formulas 125–130) and 25 (Formulas 133–135), respectively.

The condensation of CZA with benzaldehydes and Mannich reactions 44 generates, for instance, 3-benzal derivative 136 (cf. Ref. 33) and aminoalcohol 137, respectively (Scheme 26 ). The much more complex reaction with p-dimethylaminobenzaldehyde gives dyes 25 (see Scheme 5). 48 Bimolecular reactions of CZA can, on the one hand, connect two rings by use of β-ethoxyacroleinacetal to form trimethine dye 138. 143 On the other hand, copper-catalyzed dehydrogenative CC coupling and rearrangement produced a strange complex 139. 66

In a bacterial oxidation reaction Pseudomonas indigofera connects two CZA rings in 3,3′ positions to yield blue dye indigoidine (141), 24
,
144 that could be synthesized, for instance, by oxidizing 3-amino CZA (140). 145

Different halogenation methods lead to iodide 142c, 123 trichloride 143, 146 and 5-chlorinated or 5-brominated azo dyes (e.g., 144b). 147 The nucleophilic substitution of fluorine in nitrile 96 had already been described (Section 4.3.2). 32

A principle of aminating polyfunctional aromatic compounds could be applied to the synthesis of protected amino CZA ester 145e
148 (Scheme 28 ) by magnesiation (to give 145a), forming amidocuprate 145c, oxidative coupling (to give 145d), and desilylation. Analogous 3-amino compound 19b (obtained by deprotecting 19a) was transformed to sulfonamides 146a and b. 22

A second (5-position) amino group was introduced into 3-amino CZA (30a) by means of azo coupling followed by hydrogenolysis of azo dye 148 to obtain 3-amido-5-amino compound 149
120 (Scheme 29 ). Another amination method produced homologous substance 151 by nitrosating CZA 30a and reducing 5-nitroso derivative 150. 33 Condensation of amine 151 and trione 152 led to blue azoxonol dye 153 (cf. Ref. 149).

CZA and diazonium salts 154 are coupled to produce azo dyes 155a
38 and b
50 (Scheme 30 ). Other examples include thiazolylazo (156), 41 phthalimidoazo (157), 61 and 3-phenyl-azo-5-amido compounds (148). 120 Sometimes the dyes were formulated as hydrazones (e.g., 158). 34 The azo coupling kinetics were extensively explained. 14
,
150

Azo dyes (e.g., 158) may undergo a second azo coupling to form, for instance, bis-azo dye 159a. 54 These reactions can be accompanied by a recoupling, that is a partial replacement of the previously existing azo group by a second molecule of the reactand (to form 159b). The kinetics were studied. 34
,
151 Another type of bis-azo compounds (e.g., 161) is obtained when coupling CZA and diazotized amino azo dyes (e.g., 160). 62

The reductive ring cleavage (Scheme 31 ) of CZA and subsequent in situ esterification gives rise to ester 162a of 3-carboxyglutaric acid. 152 On the other hand, in the proposed pathway of two different bacterial oxidation reactions (cf. Section 2.4) intermediate CZA is split to leave 2-oxoglutaric acid (163) 28 or fumaric acid (164), 25 respectively.
Product 139 of a partial recyclization (Section 4.5.1) is presumed to be formed by the addition of a carbon-negative CZA anion to a second CZA molecule and subsequent rearrangement. 66

In the course of anellation reactions the cyclization of nitroso derivative 150 affords fused isoxazolone 165, whereas amine 151 can be oxidized to give a mixture (1:2) of trione 152 and a blue tricyclic bis-compound identical with natural lemonierin (166). 33 Furanone- and pyrrolone-fused substances 25a and b
48 have already been mentioned (Section 3.4).
Hydrogenation of CZA sodium salt affords 3-carboxyglutarimide (162b). 153

ESR studies of azasemiquinone free radical intermediates during the oxidation of CZA were described. 154 The formation of nitrogen oxides from fuel-nitrogen through HCN and NH3 was investigated by means of model compounds (among them CZA) under both inert and oxidizing conditions. 155
The radiolysis of CZA in aqueous solution produces radicals, caused by primary and secondary reactions of hydroxyl radicals and detected by ESR. 156

CZA serves as a precursor in the syntheses of a large number of interesting substances, such as, to list some examples, mappinine (167) 137 and inhibitor 168
101 (Scheme 32 ) as well as quaterpyridines (via 90) 83 or analogs of anti-tuberculosis drug bedaquiline. 96

CZA derivatives 114 and 151 are intermediates of the preparation of (S)-(−)-acromelobic acid (169) 157 and bacterial pigment lemonierin (166), 33 respectively. The synthesis of antimicrobial agent 170 is based on dichloride 40. 158 Finally, protected CZA ester 87 was dye-labeled and cyclized by use of a bis-pentafluophenyl ester to provide a voluminous macrocyclus. 122

Apart from their role as precursors, CZA itself and its direct derivatives are used or tested in many different ways (cf. Ref. 40). In the field of bioactivities (Scheme 33 ), the inhibitor activities of CZA, 159
,
160 3-substituted derivatives 136 and 137, 44 and bis-aryl ethers (such as 171
161 and symmetrically substituted analogs 79
,
128
,
162
,
163 ) were dealt with.
From theoretical (see Section 3.1) and experimental studies on CZA and its docking ability it was concluded that the substance may effectively contribute to the treatment of tuberculosis. 29

CZA diazaspiroalkaneamides (e.g., 172) were claimed to be useful against asthma and similar diseases. 164 Finally, CZA-containing peptide 173 displays a high affinity to specific domains on other peptides. 165

CZA was found to inhibit algal growth 166 and to show medium nematicidal activity. 167 CZA nitrile bis-ethers (e.g., 95) 129 including the respective bis-phenylethers (cf. Ref. 168) were claimed as herbicides.
CZA was included in the determination of the toxicity and repellency of chemicals to birds 169 and mice. 170

CZA and melamine can be used as components forming a microporous, amorphous material. 171 On the other hand, the combination of CZA and melamine 172 or cytosine, 173 developing hydrogen-bonded assemblies, are useful for iodine absorption.

CZA was also claimed as an ingredient of ceramic ink 174 and of chromium-based color passivation solution. 175 Its hindered piperidyl esters (e.g., 174, Scheme 34 ) were found to stabilize synthetic resins to light and weathering. 176
CZA tetraalkylammonium salt 27b in an ionic liquid serves as an active adsorbent and photosensitizer in the visible light-driven photoreduction of CO2 to CH4 over TiO2 catalyst. 53

Moreover, CZA was claimed to catalyze bulk polymerization of caprolactone, 177 to be a component of liquid epoxy resins showing good curability, 178 and to prevent scale during suspension polymerization of vinyl chloride. 179

A carbon paste electrode was modified by electro-reduced CZA and found to possess strong catalytic activity on the electro-oxidation of dopamine and uric acid. 180 CZA was claimed as an ingredient of electrolytic solutions with improved withstanding voltage for capacitors 181 and as brightening agent in electroplating solutions for silver. 182

CZA azo dyes (see Section 4.5.4) have attracted much attention, for instance, the reactive dye Ostazine Yellow S-7G (175) 183 (cf. Ref. 14). Other azo dyes (e.g., 155b) were applied to synthetic fibers using supercritical CO2 as medium. 184
There are patent claims regarding CZA tris-azo dyes for ink-jet printing 185 and symmetrical CZA trimethine dye 138 for non-oxidative hair colorants. 186

The acid had been applied in different ways in the classical analogous photography. It had been claimed, for instance, as coupler 187 (cf. Ref. 188), as decoloration decreasing agent in the bleach-fixing bath, 189 and as additive for improving rapid processing. 190 Azo dye 176 was found useful as filter dye. 191

CZA can be used for the spectrophotometric determination of Fe(III), U(IV), Cu(II), and V(V) 192
,
193 as well as (indirectly) of Cr(VI). 194 Nitrite is determined through the reaction with p-aminoacetophenone and coupling of the obtained diazonium salt with CZA. 195 Diazotation of the respective amino groups and coupling with CZA is also proposed for the determination of a number of chemotherapeutics 196 and (after preceding reduction) of Nimodipine, 197 Flutamide, 198 and Nitrazepam. 199
CZA-functionalized gold nanoparticles can be applied to detect Cr(III), 200 and CZA-modified carbon nanotubes (78) showed dual H2O2 and glucose detection. 112
Indirect methods of determining citrate make use of its chemical transformation to CZA ammonium salt and fluorimetry. 201–203

Another indirect application of CZA has frequently been dealt with during the last decade: The acid has been identified as the source of the blue emission of carbon dots made from citric acid and a nitrogen donor, for instance, ammonia or urea. Possible reaction paths and the structure of H-bonded clusters were discussed in detail (e.g., 42
,
204–208 ). The structural analysis is mostly based on UV and photoluminescence spectra, additionally on NMR 209 and mass spectra. 210
Finally, the preparation of luminescent carbon nanofibers by template-assisted microwave pyrolysis of citric acid and polyethyleneimine leads to CZA-type functionalities in the fibers. 211
